summary: Gruber argues that Apple Mail's only defense against email tracking pixels — disabling all remote image loading — is woefully inadequate compared to what competitors like Hey offer. He rebuts readers who pointed to this existing setting, explaining it's an all-or-nothing approach that breaks most emails visually. He highlights Hey's multi-layered approach of identifying known trackers, stripping suspicious pixels, and proxying remaining images as the model Apple should follow. He calls for Apple to add intelligent tracker blocking and image proxying to Mail, matching the sophistication of Safari's web tracking defenses.
key_points: Apple Mail's 'don't load remote content' setting is a blunt instrument that breaks email rendering rather than intelligently blocking trackers
Hey's approach of identifying known spy pixels, stripping suspicious 1x1 images, and proxying all remaining images through their servers is the gold standard
The disparity between Safari's sophisticated tracking protections and Mail's complete lack of them is indefensible for a privacy-focused company
Email tracking reveals not just whether you opened a message, but when and where you were via reverse IP geolocation
The root cause traces back to email adopting HTML — email clients are effectively web browsers without any browser privacy protections
money_quotes: No one would call it a good solution to tracking if Safari's only defense were an option not to load any images at all until you manually click a button in each tab to load them all. But that's exactly what Apple offers with Mail.
It's a disgraceful practice that has grown to be accepted industry-wide as standard procedure, because the vast majority of users have no idea it's even going on.
Through reverse IP address geolocation, newsletter and marketing email services track not just that you opened their messages, but when you opened them, and where you were.
Apple should offer defenses against email tracking just as robust as Safari's defenses against web tracking.
Don't get me started on how predictable this entire privacy disaster was, once we lost the war over whether email messages should be plain text only or could contain embedded HTML.
Effectively all email clients are web browsers now, yet don't have any of the privacy protection features actual browsers do.
key_insight: Apple's email privacy protections are embarrassingly far behind its browser privacy protections, and the company should bring the same intelligent tracker-blocking philosophy from Safari to Mail.
